name: Nearmate
tagline: Meet nearby people for specific moments.
description: Nearmate is a hyperlocal social networking app that connects people who need something small, immediate, and human from others nearby. Instead of trying to replace your broader social life, it solves the awkward gap between strangers and friends: finding someone to lend a charger, walk to a new neighborhood café, proofread a slide deck, share a rideshare, or simply eat lunch with today. Users post a “moment” describing a concrete need, time window, and comfort level, and the app matches them with nearby people who are available, trusted, and relevant. This helps in daily life by turning ordinary frictions—loneliness, last-minute logistics, unsafe solo situations, and wasted time between events—into low-pressure opportunities to connect. For example, a new resident could find a local to explain transit routes, a remote worker could find a co-working buddy for an afternoon, or a traveler could meet someone to split an airport taxi. What makes Nearmate unique is that it is built around short-lived, task-based social intent rather than profiles and followers, so interactions feel practical, timely, and less performative than existing social platforms.
features: Moment Posts let users publish a specific need with a time limit, location radius, and interaction style, which keeps requests concrete and easy to respond to.
Trust Modes allow users to choose between anonymous chat, verified profiles, or mutual-interest matching, so each interaction feels safe for the situation.
Micro-Intent Matching uses context like availability, shared neighborhood habits, and requested help type to suggest people who are likely to be genuinely useful.
Meetup Anchors recommend nearby public places such as libraries, cafés, or transit hubs to make spontaneous meetups safer and easier to coordinate.
After-Action Circles let users leave lightweight feedback and follow-up invites, helping useful one-off encounters turn into reliable local connections over time.
audience: Young professionals, students, newcomers to a city, frequent travelers, and remote workers ages 18–40 who often need quick, local, low-commitment social connections. It is especially useful in urban environments where people are surrounded by others but still feel disconnected.
useCases: Sarah just finished a stressful meeting and needs a quiet place to work, so she posts a one-hour moment asking for a nearby café buddy who also likes focused work sessions. Within minutes, she finds another remote worker heading to the same neighborhood.

Jamal arrived in a new city for a two-week contract and wants a quick orientation, so he uses Nearmate to find someone who can walk him through the safest route to the train station and explain local lunch spots. He avoids hours of searching online and gets practical help from a local in real time.

Mina missed her last bus after a concert and doesn't want to wait alone late at night, so she opens the app to find someone nearby heading in the same direction or willing to share a ride to the transit hub. The app gives her a safer, less isolating way to handle an unexpectedly stressful moment.
